notes: Premier prairie fruit shrub. Extremely hardy. Fruit similar to blueberry. Self-fertile but better yield with cross-pollination.
Fruit must be cooked/processed (raw pits contain cyanogenic glycosides). Makes excellent syrup and jelly. Important bird food.
Fast-growing pioneer species. Small tart fruit good for jelly. Short-lived (30 yrs) but excellent nurse tree.
Excellent edible groundcover. Spreads by runners. Intense flavour. Leaf tea is traditional.
Leaf tea is traditional for women's health. Spreads vigorously by suckers. Fruit identical to cultivated raspberry.
Low trailing raspberry relative. Prefers moist woodland edges. Small sweet fruit.
Large maple-shaped leaves. Soft delicate fruit doesn't store well but excellent fresh. Thornless.
Thorny shrub. Fruit excellent for preserves and pies. Tolerates shade well.
Strong-flavoured black currant. High vitamin C. Traditional medicine for sore throat. Prefers moist areas.
Trailing currant of moist forests. Tart fruit good for jelly and sauce.
Prickly stems. Small dark berries. Prefers cool moist sites. Good wildlife plant.
Not a true cranberry. Fruit best after frost. Makes excellent jelly. Showy white flower clusters.
Mooseberry. Smaller than highbush. Fruit tart, good cooked. Very hardy understory shrub.
Kinnikinnick. Excellent evergreen groundcover. Fruit bland but edible. Leaf tea traditional for urinary tract. Fixes sandy soils.
Lingonberry relative. Requires acidic soil. Small tart berries excellent in preserves.
Wild blueberry. Requires acidic soil. Low spreading shrub. Sweet berries.
Bog habitat. Blue-black berries slightly larger than V. myrtilloides. Needs consistently moist acidic soil.
Alberta's floral emblem (shared with pasqueflower). Rosehips extremely high in vitamin C. Important pollinator.
Low spreading rose. Long bloom period. Smaller hips than R. acicularis but still edible.
One of most versatile edible wilds. Young shoots like asparagus, roots starchy, pollen as flour. Needs standing water or saturated soil.
Beautiful drooping flower heads. Entire plant edible with mild onion flavour. Excellent pollinator.

Native variety is var. sibiricum (not the European var. schoenoprasum). Ensure nursery stock is native provenance. Native form of common chives. Purple globe flowers excellent pollinator. All parts edible.
Outstanding nutrient accumulator. Young leaves cooked like spinach (cooking neutralizes sting). Rich in iron and minerals. Traditional medicine.
Only mint species native to North America. Spreads aggressively. Tea and culinary. Natural pest repellent.
Sunflower relative. Starchy tubers (inulin-rich). Extremely productive. Can be invasive — plant with containment strategy.
Anise-scented. Top-tier pollinator plant. Excellent tea. Edible flowers. Self-seeds readily.
Bee balm. One of the best native pollinator plants. Earl Grey-like tea. Traditional cold/flu remedy. Drought tolerant.
Pioneer species. Young shoots like asparagus. Flower tea. Major honey plant. Indicator of disturbed soil.
Spreading groundcover. Starchy roots were important Indigenous food. Silver-backed leaves.
Woodland plant. Root tea traditional tonic. Shade tolerant. Blue-black berries for wildlife (not palatable to humans).
Early spring ephemeral. Small corms taste like potato. Delicate pink-white flowers. Goes dormant by summer.
Silver aromatic foliage. Traditional seasoning and smudge. Extremely drought tolerant.
Buds produce balm of Gilead — anti-inflammatory resin. Fast-growing windbreak. Cottonwood relative.
Most widely distributed tree in North America. Clonal colonies. Inner bark is survival food. Pioneer species.
Sap tapped like maple for syrup (lower sugar content). Bark for crafts. Beautiful white bark.
Only native nut tree in Alberta. Small but flavourful hazelnuts. Understory shrub. Very early catkin pollen source.
Traditional wound herb (styptic). Anti-inflammatory tea. Accumulates potassium, phosphorus, copper. Top pollinator.
White sage of the prairies. Ceremonial smudge plant. Silver foliage. Digestive tea. Spreads by rhizome.
Berry-like cones used as spice and gin flavouring. Traditional respiratory and urinary remedy. Extremely hardy.
Prostrate evergreen groundcover. Similar medicinal uses to common juniper. Excellent erosion control.
Traditional tea plant. Use in moderation — contains ledol. Bog/peatland plant. Leathery leaves with rusty underside.
Sacred ceremonial grass. Vanilla-like scent when dried. Culturally significant. Needs regular harvesting to stay vigorous.
Legume — fixes nitrogen. Root has sweet licorice flavour. Traditional sore throat remedy. Bur-like seed pods.
Traditional expectorant. Root harvested for respiratory medicine. Small inconspicuous flowers. Becoming uncommon.
Sweet flag. Wetland plant. Root chewed for sore throat and digestive aid. Culturally significant in many Indigenous traditions.
Resin blisters on bark yield balsam — traditional respiratory remedy and wound sealant. Aromatic needles for tea.
Needle tea rich in vitamin C. Pitch used as wound sealant and chewing gum. Dominant boreal tree.
Bog/muskeg species. Similar medicinal uses to white spruce. Slower growing. Root used for lacing birchbark.
Only native deciduous conifer. Golden fall colour. Bark tea traditional for respiratory ailments. Tolerates wet soils.
Striking red winter stems. Traditional smoking-mix bark. Anti-diarrheal. Berries for wildlife. Excellent riparian plant.
Diamond willow. Bark contains salicin (aspirin precursor). Traditional pain remedy. Fast-growing riparian species.
Soapberry. Fruit whipped into foamy soapolallie (Indian ice cream). Nitrogen-fixing actinorrhizal plant. Bitter raw.
Thorny. Silver leaves. Tart berries excellent after frost. Nitrogen fixer. Good hedge/windbreak.
Major nitrogen fixer. Riparian species. Bark astringent used traditionally. Excellent restoration plant.
Dense thicket-forming shrub. White berries persist into winter. Medicinal use external only — berries mildly toxic internally. Good for erosion.
Traditional wound herb. Low spreading pollinator. Tea for sore throat. Can function as lawn alternative.
Arrow-shaped leaves. Flowers before leaves appear. Traditional cough remedy. Colonizes wet disturbed ground.
Root is traditional sedative. Sweet-scented flowers attract pollinators. Prefers moist meadows.
Critical late-season pollinator. Does NOT cause hay fever (wind-borne ragweed does). Spreads by rhizome.
Flat-topped flower clusters. More compact than Canada goldenrod. Drought tolerant.
Tall perennial sunflower. Seeds edible. Major late-season pollinator and bird food.
Tall late-blooming sunflower. Very drought tolerant. Seeds for birds and humans. Roots historically eaten.
Native wild form. Seeds edible. Iconic pollinator. Self-seeds annually.
Classic prairie wildflower. Long bloom period. Self-seeds freely. Excellent for pollinator gardens.
Long-blooming prairie wildflower. Very drought tolerant. Red and yellow bicolour flowers.
Distinctive columnar seedhead. Drooping yellow or maroon petals. Very drought tolerant.
Blue-purple flowers. Critical late-season pollinator. Drought tolerant.
Woodland aster. Blue-violet flowers. Tolerates partial shade. Good for forest edge plantings.
Large violet flower heads. Woodland edge species. Good late-summer colour.
Blue-purple daisy-like flowers. Long bloom. Good cut flower.
Alberta's floral emblem. Among the first spring flowers. Fuzzy purple cups. Do not transplant from wild — use nursery stock.
White flowers. Vigorous spreader — good groundcover but can be aggressive. Moist meadows.
Tiny early pollinator. Edible flowers and leaves. Larval host for fritillary butterflies.
White violet with yellow centre. Shade tolerant woodland species. Edible flowers.
Delicate blue bells on wiry stems. Extremely long bloom period. Rocky/sandy soils. Very hardy.
Sky-blue flowers open daily and drop. Fibre plant historically. Excellent in dry meadow plantings.
Waxy blue-green leaves. Bright blue-purple tubular flowers. Excellent for dry sunny sites. Early bloom.
Slender lilac-flowered beardtongue. Dry prairie sites. Important for native bees.
Nitrogen-fixing legume. White cylindrical flower spikes. Deep taproot makes it very drought tolerant.
Purple cylindrical flower heads. Nitrogen fixer. Deep-rooted prairie plant. Tea from leaves.
Yellow lupine-like flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Early prairie bloomer. Seeds toxic — not edible. Pioneer species.
Climbing/trailing legume. Purple-blue flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Good for mixed plantings.
Cream-white flower spikes. Nitrogen fixer. Deep-rooted. Good restoration plant.
Dense purple flower spikes. Silvery woolly foliage. Dry grasslands. Toxic to livestock but good pollinator plant.
Pink nodding flowers become feathery seedheads (the smoke). Early pollinator. Excellent dry garden plant.
White flowers with pink veins. Woodland edge and meadow. Long bloom period.
Sticky glandular stems. Pink-purple flowers. Meadow and grassland species.
Clusters of tiny white flowers. Square stems. Traditional dye plant. Good ground-layer pollinator.
Not a grass — iris family. Small blue star flowers. Charming in meadow plantings. Short-lived but self-seeds.
Cyclamen-like pink flowers with reflexed petals. Moist meadows and stream edges. Spring ephemeral.
Spectacular orange-red upfacing lily. Increasingly rare — never harvest from wild. Buy nursery-propagated.
Woodland groundcover. Star-shaped white flowers. Red-striped berries for wildlife. Shade tolerant.
Tiny white flower clusters. Woodland groundcover. Red berries in late summer (mildly toxic). Spreads by rhizome.
Miniature dogwood. White 'flowers' (bracts). Red berry clusters edible but bland. Excellent shade groundcover.
Delicate paired pink bells. Trailing evergreen groundcover. Named by Linnaeus as his favourite plant.
Blue and white spurred flowers. Hummingbird-pollinated. Woodland edge. Self-seeds.
Native coral bells relative. Greenish flowers on tall stalks. Rosette-forming. Dry to mesic sites.
Brilliant red bracts. Semi-parasitic on grass roots — needs host plants. Hummingbird magnet. Difficult to transplant.
Massive pink flower heads. Major butterfly plant. Prefers moist to wet soils. Late-season nectar source.
White fuzzy flower clusters. Moist meadows and stream edges. Long bloom. Good bee shrub.
Yellow flowers bloom all summer. Extremely long bloom period. Very hardy and drought tolerant. Good low hedge.
Monarch butterfly host plant. Large pink flower clusters. Milky sap. Critical conservation plant.
Purple spike blooms top-down. Late season pollinator. Deep taproot. Very drought tolerant. Traditional digestive remedy.

BORDERLINE FOR EDMONTON: More reliably found in southern Alberta river valleys. Zone 5a+ typically; may struggle in Edmonton's zone 3b-4a. Vigorous native vine. White flower clusters. Fluffy seedheads. Only native clematis on prairies.
Distinctive eyelash-like seedheads. Premier native lawn/meadow grass. Extremely drought tolerant. Warm season.
Keystone grass of northern fescue prairie. Cool season bunchgrass. Indicator of undisturbed prairie.
Tall warm-season grass. Excellent for biomass and erosion control. Golden fall colour. Wildlife cover.
Short-lived bunchgrass. Quick establishing — good for initial restoration seedings. Self-seeds.
Tall bunchgrass with nodding seedheads. Fast establishing. Good for restoration and shelterbelts.
Ancient plant. High silica content — traditional scouring. Accumulates minerals. Spreads aggressively. Not for casual planting.
Wolf willow. Intensely fragrant silver flowers. Nitrogen fixer. Dry mealy fruit edible but not palatable. Excellent for reclamation.
Bright yellow early flowers. MUST be thoroughly cooked before eating (toxic raw). Wet meadows and stream edges.
Very early catkins — first major pollen source for bees. Bark contains salicin. Fast-growing riparian shrub.
White ornamental berries persist into winter. Bird food. Shade tolerant. Traditional external medicine only.
Also called old-field goldenrod. Compact goldenrod. One-sided flower clusters. Very drought tolerant. Dry sandy soils.

BORDERLINE FOR EDMONTON: Native to Alberta per VASCAN but primarily found in southern grassland region. May be difficult to source for Edmonton. Greenish-white flower clusters. Monarch host plant. Drought tolerant dry prairie species.
Small multi-stemmed birch of stream banks. Sap drinkable. Reddish-brown bark. Good riparian restoration tree.
Alpine strawberry type. Smaller fruit than F. virginiana but more aromatic. Shade tolerant groundcover.
